front: Poetry Collection: opens with "To the Reader," which addresses the "hypocrite reader" as "my double, my brother" and describes Ennui as a monster who "dreams of scaffolds and smokes his hookah pipe"
Poetry Collection: sections include "Spleen and Ideal," "Wine," "Revolt," and "Death"; dedicated to Theophile Gautier, "perfect magician of French letters"
Poetry Collection: six poems were censored from the first edition, including "Lesbos," "The Damned Women," and "The Jewels"; later republished in Les Epaves
Poetry Collection: "Tableaux Parisiens" (Parisian Scenes) was added during Paris's Haussmann renovation; 18 poems comprise a 24-hour cycle critiquing the renovated city
Poem: compares the Poet to the title bird, a "prince of the skies" whose large wings drag like oars when captured; the bird is "comic" and "ugly"
Poem: opens with "Andromache, I think of you!"; dedicated to Victor Hugo; laments that a city's face "changes faster than a human heart"
Poem: the title bird escapes its cage and cries "homesick for his fair native lake: Rain, when will you fall? Thunder, when will you roll?"
Essay: examines Constantin Guys ("Monsieur G") as a "passionate spectator" and "eternal convalescent" for whom "the crowd is his element"
work: The Painter of Modern Life / The Flaneur

front: Essay: originated the archetype of the flaneur; coined the term "modernity"; the flaneur is "a prince who is everywhere in possession of his incognito"
work: The Painter of Modern Life / The Flaneur

front: Poet: Walter Benjamin expanded this author's flaneur concept in the Arcades Project, calling him a "secret agent" of bourgeois discontent and theorizing a "commodity-soul"
work: The Painter of Modern Life / The Flaneur

front: Essay: distinguishes "ladies" from "fallen women" in its "Women and Prostitutes" section; expanded by Griselda Pollock in "Modernity and the Spaces of Femininity"
work: The Painter of Modern Life / The Flaneur

front: Poem: beseeches the "Wisest of Angels" to "take pity on my long despair" after every couplet; concludes with a prayer to rest by the Tree of Knowledge
Poem: describes nature as a "temple" with "living pillars" and a "forest of symbols"; the Symbolist movement took its name from this poem
Poem: begins "My child, my sister, think of the rapture of living together there!"; envisions "ornate ceilings" and "limpid mirrors"; ends with the word "volupte"
Poetry Collection: prose poems inspired by Aloysius Bertrand's Gaspard de la Nuit; includes "Be Drunk" and "Let's Beat Up the Poor!"
Poem: the narrator has a fistfight with a beggar outside a bar to determine if he is worthy of charity
Poem: depicts a sickly "old man with tattered yellow clothes"; the speaker's soul becomes "a ship without a mast on a monstrous, borderless sea" after encountering identical hobbled men
Poem: eight quatrains ("medallions") about the art of Rubens, Da Vinci, Rembrandt, and Michelangelo
Poem: travelers discover "an oasis of horror in a desert of boredom"
Poem: sexualizes a corpse "blossoming like a flower" as "black battalions" of creatures emerge from it
Poem: the speaker imagines living "like a voluptuous cat at the feet of a queen" near the title figure
Poem: the speaker makes fleeting eye contact with a passing woman; concludes "O you that I had loved, O you who knew it!"
Poet: spent twenty years translating Edgar Allan Poe into French; supposedly learned English for that purpose; popularized Poe throughout Europe
Poet: Algernon Swinburne wrote the elegy "Ave Atque Vale" for this poet, titled in reference to Catullus
Poet: close friend of Edouard Manet; posed for Courbet's The Artist's Studio; savaged the mechanical nature of photography
Poet: his half-Haitian mistress Jeanne Duval was fictionalized in Nalo Hopkinson's The Salt Roads
Poet: wrote "Richard Wagner and Tannhauser in Paris" despite having little musical training
Poet: inspired Lemony Snicket's choice of the surname "Baudelaire" for the orphans in A Series of Unfortunate Events
Poet: wrote "On the Essence of Laughter" about a Pierrot robbing a cleaning woman in pantomime
